message.content:
Neapolitan Pizza Dough (makes 4 balls, ~250–270 g each for 12–14" pizzas)

Ingredients (by weight – use a scale for accuracy):

1,000 g Italian Tipo 00 flour (12–13% protein; bread flour as backup)
600 g cool water (60% hydration; 18–22°C / 64–72°F – adjust slightly cooler in hot weather)
25 g fine sea salt
1 g instant dry yeast (¼ tsp) or 3 g fresh yeast (use 0.5 g instant for 48+ hour cold ferment)

No oil, sugar, or other additions – that’s the authentic rule.

Step-by-step process

Dissolve salt (2 min)
Pour the water into a large bowl. Add salt and stir until completely dissolved.
Mix/autolyse (25–30 min)
Add 900 g flour and mix by hand (or spoon) until no dry bits remain – it will look shaggy and sticky. Cover and rest 20–30 min. (This step improves gluten and flavor.)
Add yeast and finish mixing (3 min)
Sprinkle the yeast over the dough (no need to pre-dissolve instant). Add the remaining 100 g flour and mix until incorporated.
Knead (8–12 min by hand or 5–7 min in stand mixer)
Use slap-and-fold or stretch-and-fold in the bowl. The dough should become smooth, soft, and slightly tacky – it should pass the windowpane test (stretch a small piece thin without tearing). Stop here; do not develop bread-like tightness.
Bulk fermentation (1.5–2 hours at 20–24°C)
Cover tightly. Let rise until volume increases ~50–100% and the surface looks bubbly. Do not let it double.
Divide and pre-shape (10 min)
Turn dough onto a lightly floured counter. Divide into 4 equal pieces. Gently fold edges to center, flip seam-down, and rotate to create light surface tension. Cover and rest 15–20 min.
Final shaping and proofing (choose your fermentation option)
Same-day room-temp (total 8–12 hours from start): Form tight balls, place in a lightly floured or oiled covered container. Proof another 6–8 hours at room temp until light, airy, and slow to spring back when poked.
Cold ferment (best flavor, 24–72 hours): After pre-shape rest (or final balling), put balls in a sealed container in the fridge (4°C). Use between 24–48 hours for peak results; up to 72 hours max. Pull out 1–2 hours before stretching to warm and relax.
Hybrid: 2 hours room-temp bulk, then fridge the balls.
Ready to use
Dough is perfect when it feels light and puffy. Bring chilled balls to room temp before stretching. Stretch gently by hand (no rolling pin) on a floured surface just before baking.

Total timing summary

Hands-on: 30–45 min
Minimum: 8 hours (room temp)
Recommended: 24–48 hours cold ferment

Common mistakes to avoid

Too much yeast → overproofed, collapsed, sour-tasting dough.
All-purpose flour → weak structure, tears when stretched.
Over-kneading → tough, dense crust instead of open, airy cornicione.
Skipping rest after dividing → dough snaps back when you try to stretch it.
Hot water → kills yeast.
Letting dough dry out or overproof → cover well and watch timing.
Stretching cold dough → rips and uneven base.

Follow these steps exactly and you’ll get the classic Neapolitan puffy rim, leopard-spotted char, and light digestible crumb when baked at 450–500°C (840–930°F) for 60–90 seconds. Scale the recipe up or down as needed – the ratios stay the same. Let me know if you want adjustments for sourdough version or specific oven setup.
